title: CV
text:
I am trained in human geography, with research interests in scientific mobility, regional innovation, urban networks, and empirical work using longitudinal relational data. This page provides a concise overview; the public PDF contains the full CV.




Public CV in PDF format.

Download CV






M.Sc. in Human Geography, 2023-2026. School of Geographic Sciences; economic geography and regional innovation.



B.Sc. in Human Geography and Urban-Rural Planning, 2019-2023. Training in quantitative geography, GIS applications, urban geography, regional analysis, and planning.







Spatial Structure and Determinants of Top Global Biomedical Scientists’ Mobility from an Urban Network Perspective — accepted article.
Intercity Mobility Network of Top Global Biomedical Scientists: A Dual Perspective of Whole and Ego Networks — master’s thesis.
Author-Institution Linkage and Geocoding Feasibility Audit — public code repository.






Python; R; Stata; GIS; social network analysis; ego-network analysis; conditional logit models; ERGM/WERGM; entity resolution; longitudinal multi-source data construction; coverage audits.

title: Projects
Research
text:
My research sits at the intersection of economic geography, urban and regional economics, and science of science. I study how scientific mobility, institutional affiliations, collaboration relations, and city-level opportunity structures are associated with the spatial organization of research and innovation.
The common thread is empirical work that treats scientists, institutions, publications, and cities as linked but distinct units of analysis. This helps separate observed affiliations, employment histories, collaboration ties, and mobility events before applying statistical or network models.






I examine how elite biomedical scientists move across institutions and cities over time. Current work links career histories with publication-location exposure and destination choices while avoiding claims that exceed the available observational design.



I study how cities and regions differ in research capacity, organizational diversity, and positions within mobility and collaboration networks. This work connects urban opportunity structures with observed patterns of high-skilled mobility and knowledge production.



I build and audit datasets that connect scientists, institutions, publications, and places. The goal is to make relational data usable for research on scientific geography without obscuring uncertainty in linkage, coverage, or geocoding.







Longitudinal scientist-institution-city data
Discrete-choice models
Network analysis
Spatial data methods
Reproducible workflows in Python and R








This project studies whether a scientist’s pre-move publication-location exposure is associated with the destination city selected from an event-specific candidate set.



This line of work extends earlier thesis and article research on intercity mobility networks by developing longitudinal scientist-institution-city data and directed weighted network indicators.



The public repository uses synthetic records to demonstrate schema validation, institution normalization, geocoding checks, and documentation patterns for reproducible research workflows.

title: Ran Wang
text:
Urban & Regional Economics · Scientific Mobility · Regional Innovation


I am an empirical researcher trained in economic geography. I study scientific mobility, institutional platforms, and regional innovation using longitudinal data on scientists, institutions, publications, and cities. My work combines discrete-choice models, network analysis, and spatial data methods.


View Research Download CV Selected Projects GitHub



Photo: John Rodenn Castillo / Unsplash

title: Ran Wang
text:
Research Focus

My research examines how scientific careers, institutional platforms, and city-level opportunity structures shape the geography of knowledge production. I focus on careful data construction, transparent definitions of mobility and affiliation, and empirical designs that connect individual trajectories with urban and regional innovation systems.

title: Ran Wang
text:
Research Themes



Mobility


Scientific Mobility and Destination Choice
I study how scientists move across institutions and cities, with attention to the information and opportunity structures visible before a move. Current work uses event-specific destination-choice data to compare observed moves with plausible alternative destinations.




Regions


Regional Innovation and Urban Networks
This work examines how cities and regions accumulate research capacity through institutions, collaboration ties, and mobility flows. The emphasis is on measured associations between network position, urban opportunity, and innovation-related activity.




Methods


Relational Data and Network Methods
I build scientist-institution-city datasets that make affiliations, publications, collaboration ties, and mobility events analytically distinct. This supports network analysis, spatial comparison, and reproducible workflows for auditing source coverage.

title: Ran Wang
text:
Selected Work



Accepted Article


Spatial Structure and Determinants of Top Global Biomedical Scientists’ Mobility from an Urban Network Perspective
A related Chinese article studies the spatial structure and determinants of top global biomedical scientists’ mobility from an urban network perspective. Final publication metadata will be added after confirmation.




Working Paper


Pre-move Publication-Location Exposure and Destination Choice among Elite Biomedical Scientists
This working paper examines how pre-move publication-location exposure is associated with observed destination choice among candidate cities. Restricted source data and full manuscript materials are not publicly released here.




Public Repository


Author-Institution Linkage and Geocoding Feasibility Audit
A public repository demonstrates synthetic author-institution-city linkage, geocoding QA, validation checks, duplicate diagnostics, and reproducible documentation without redistributing restricted bibliographic data.
View repository
